From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [National Historic Site (United States)](/w/index.php?title=National_Historic_Site_(United_States)&redirect=no "National Historic Site (United States)"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Salem_Customs_House.jpg/250px-Salem_Customs_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:Salem_Customs_House.jpg)

[Salem Maritime National Historical Park](/wiki/Salem_Maritime_National_Historical_Park "Salem Maritime National Historical Park") in [Salem, Massachusetts](/wiki/Salem,_Massachusetts "Salem, Massachusetts"), was the first national historic site to be established in the U.S.

National Historic Sites (NHSes) and National Historical Parks (NHPs) are officially recognized areas of nationally historic significance in the United States. They are usually owned and managed by the federal government. An NHS usually contains a single historical feature directly associated with its subject, while an NHP is an area that generally extends beyond single properties or buildings to include a mix of historic and later structures and sometimes significant natural features.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

There are 64 NHPs and 85 NHSes. Most NHPs and NHSes are managed by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") (NPS). Some federally designated sites are owned by local authorities or privately owned, but are authorized to request assistance from the NPS as affiliated areas.[[2]](#cite_note-2) One property is managed by the [U.S. Forest Service](/wiki/United_States_Forest_Service "United States Forest Service"): [Grey Towers National Historic Site](/wiki/Grey_Towers_National_Historic_Site "Grey Towers National Historic Site").[[3]](#cite_note-uniqueness_explained-3)

Since October 15, 1966, all historic areas in the NPS, including NHPs and NHSes, are automatically listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") (NRHP). There are about 90,000 NRHP sites, the large majority of which are neither owned nor managed by the NPS. Of these, about 2,600 have been designated at the highest status as [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") (NHL) sites.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_national_historic_sites_and_historical_parks_of_the_United_States&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

After its founding in 1916, the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service") initially oversaw sites of primarily scenic and natural significance, including [national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_the_United_States "List of national parks of the United States") and [national monuments](/wiki/List_of_national_monuments_of_the_United_States "List of national monuments of the United States"). Historians soon began recommending preservation of sites relating to human history.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) Congress created [Colonial National Monument](/wiki/Colonial_National_Monument "Colonial National Monument") in 1930 to protect the [Jamestown Settlement](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia") and [Yorktown battlefield](/wiki/Siege_of_Yorktown "Siege of Yorktown") in Virginia as one of the first new historical areas, and it was renamed a national historical park in 1936.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) It then established [Morristown National Historical Park](/wiki/Morristown_National_Historical_Park "Morristown National Historical Park"), the 1779–1780 winter encampment of the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") in New Jersey, on March 2, 1933, as the first NHP: The U.S. House committee noted that the new designation was logical for the area and set a new precedent, with comparison to the [national military parks](/wiki/National_Military_Park "National Military Park"), which were then in the War Department. The park's establishment allowed the NPS to have an administrative historical program with professional historians.[[6]](#cite_note-6) President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") reorganized the agency to also oversee memorials and military parks with historic significance later in 1933, substantially broadening the NPS's mandate.[[7]](#cite_note-:22-7)

In 1935, Congress passed the [Historic Sites Act](/wiki/Historic_Sites_Act "Historic Sites Act") ([Pub. L.](/wiki/Act_of_Congress#Public_law,_private_law,_designation "Act of Congress") [49–666](https://uslaw.link/citation/us-law/public/49/666)), which established that "it is a national policy to preserve for public use historic sites, buildings and objects of national significance for the inspiration and benefit of the people of the United States."[[8]](#cite_note-8) This expanded upon the [Antiquities Act](/wiki/Antiquities_Act "Antiquities Act") of 1906, which gave the President the ability to order "the protection of objects of historic and scientific interest." The Historic Sites Act directed the National Park Service to survey historic sites which may be of national significance, as well as restore and acquire properties. The [Historic American Buildings Survey](/wiki/Heritage_Documentation_Programs#Historic_American_Buildings_Survey "Heritage Documentation Programs") began to document the country's architectural heritage and identify buildings for potential protection. Initially the [Secretary of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") could designate national historic sites, though this did not include funding for acquisition or administration without congressional action.[[7]](#cite_note-:22-7) [Salem Maritime National Historic Site](/wiki/Salem_Maritime_National_Historic_Site "Salem Maritime National Historic Site") was the first place to be preserved as a national historic site, created by Secretary [Harold L. Ickes](/wiki/Harold_L._Ickes "Harold L. Ickes")'s secretarial order on March 17, 1938.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It had followed his designation of the [Jefferson National Expansion Memorial](/wiki/Jefferson_National_Expansion_Memorial "Jefferson National Expansion Memorial") in 1935; many historic sites in the National Park System continue to be protected under different designation types.[[7]](#cite_note-:22-7) [Hopewell Furnace National Historic Site](/wiki/Hopewell_Furnace_National_Historic_Site "Hopewell Furnace National Historic Site") was designated later that year, another example of industrial heritage.

As the NPS developed criteria for nationally significant historic sites in the late 1930s, it aimed to identify unique sites that could each tell a broad story and would fit together to cover all aspects of American history. Surveys of sites were guided by themes and chronologies to ensure a diverse and comprehensive selection of those most representative of different eras and geographies, with less political influence over site selection.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10) In the 1950s, the [Mission 66](/wiki/Mission_66 "Mission 66") program revived historic studies that had lagged during World War II and saw the creation of the [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") program as a method to recognize important sites. From the 1960s to 1990s, the NPS evolved from a thematic framework, in which numerous specific themes and subthemes of American history were expected to each be included in some way in the system,[[11]](#cite_note-11) to a conceptual framework, whereby both new and existing park units would be examined more holistically for ways to study history such as "creating social movements and institutions," "developing the American economy," and "peopling places." In the 20th century, potential new park units have been recommended not so much on "an orderly, balanced, and comprehensive" preservation of "outstanding examples", as Chief Historian Ronald Lee put it, but on those mandated to be studied by Congress, most of whose requests are recommended against by the NPS.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

A 1973 NPS publication outlined policies for administration of historical areas, which were distinct from its natural and recreational areas. This included not only NHSes and NHPs but also national military parks, national battlefields, national battlefield parks, national battlefield sites, national memorials, and some national monuments; at that time there were 178 such areas, and management focused on "maintaining and where necessary restoring the historical integrity of structures, sites and objects significant to the commemoration or illustration of the historical story".[[12]](#cite_note-12) But because most units contained a combination of natural, historic, and recreational lands, the [General Authorities Act](/wiki/National_Park_Service_General_Authorities_Act "National Park Service General Authorities Act") of 1970 made all areas equal within the [National Park System](/wiki/National_Park_System "National Park System");[[13]](#cite_note-13) separate policy manuals for each were replaced in 1975 with one that would tailor policies in each park respective to the purpose of zones within.[[7]](#cite_note-:22-7)

## National Historic Sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_national_historic_sites_and_historical_parks_of_the_United_States&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: National Historic Sites")]

See also: [List of areas in the United States National Park System § National historic sites](/wiki/List_of_areas_in_the_United_States_National_Park_System#National_historic_sites "List of areas in the United States National Park System")

National Historic Sites are generally federally owned and administered properties, though some remain under private or local government ownership. There are currently 85 NHSes, of which seventy-five are official NPS units, nine are NPS affiliated areas, and one is managed by the [United States Forest Service](/wiki/United_States_Forest_Service "United States Forest Service").

Derived from the Historic Sites Act of 1935, a number of NHSes were established by [United States Secretaries of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior"), but most have been authorized by acts of [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). In 1937, the first NHS was created in [Salem, Massachusetts](/wiki/Salem,_Massachusetts "Salem, Massachusetts"), in order to preserve and interpret the maritime history of New England and the United States; in 2025 [Salem Maritime NHS](/wiki/Salem_Maritime_National_Historical_Park "Salem Maritime National Historical Park") was redesignated as an NHP.

The prison operated during the final fourteen months of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), February 1864 – April 1865. It was overcrowded, with an inadequate water supply, inadequate food, and unsanitary conditions. ### International Historic Site

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_national_historic_sites_and_historical_parks_of_the_United_States&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: International Historic Site")]

There is one International Historic Site in the US park system, a unique designation given to [Saint Croix Island](/wiki/Saint_Croix_Island,_Maine "Saint Croix Island, Maine"), Maine, on the [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick") border. The title, given to the site of the first permanent French settlement in America, recognizes the influence that it has had on both Canada and the United States. The preservation and management policies of the NPS do not distinguish between these designations.

## National Historical Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_national_historic_sites_and_historical_parks_of_the_United_States&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: National Historical Parks")]

See also: [List of areas in the United States National Park System § National historical parks](/wiki/List_of_areas_in_the_United_States_National_Park_System#National_historical_parks "List of areas in the United States National Park System")

National historical parks tend to be larger and more complex than national historic sites. In the United States, sites are "historic", while parks are "historical". The NPS explains that a site can be intrinsically historic, while a park is a modern legal invention. As such, a park is not itself "historic", but can be called "historical" when it contains historic resources. It is the resources which are historic, not the park.[[108]](#cite_note-108) There are 64 national historical parks.

| [Weir Farm](/wiki/Weir_Farm_National_Historical_Park "Weir Farm National Historical Park") |  | [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") | 74.20 acres (0.3003 km2) | [J. Alden Weir](/wiki/J._Alden_Weir "J. Alden Weir") was an [impressionist](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism") painter and founding member of ["The Ten"](/wiki/Ten_American_Painters "Ten American Painters") who lived and worked in this house and studio from 1882 until his death in 1919. The park also commemorates the lives of artists such as [John Singer Sargent](/wiki/John_Singer_Sargent "John Singer Sargent"), [Albert Pinkham Ryder](/wiki/Albert_Pinkham_Ryder "Albert Pinkham Ryder"), and several other notable artists who stayed with the Weirs family throughout their lives.[[173]](#cite_note-173) |
| [Women's Rights](/wiki/Women%27s_Rights_National_Historical_Park "Women's Rights National Historical Park") |  | [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") | 7.44 acres (0.0301 km2) | In 1848, the [Seneca Falls Convention](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_Convention "Seneca Falls Convention") was held in [Seneca Falls, New York](/wiki/Seneca_Falls,_New_York "Seneca Falls, New York"). The convention was [America's](/wiki/United_States "United States") first [women's rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights") convention, and [suffrage advocates](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_States "Women's suffrage in the United States") such as [Elizabeth Cady Stanton](/wiki/Elizabeth_Cady_Stanton "Elizabeth Cady Stanton"), [Lucretia Mott](/wiki/Lucretia_Mott "Lucretia Mott"), and [Frederick Douglass](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass "Frederick Douglass") were in attendance. The [Declaration of Sentiments](/wiki/Declaration_of_Sentiments "Declaration of Sentiments") was drafted and signed at the convention. The park preserves several sites associated with the convention, including the houses of Stanton and McClintock.[[174]](#cite_note-174) |

### International Historical Park

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=List_of_national_historic_sites_and_historical_parks_of_the_United_States&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: International Historical Park")]

Klondike Gold Rush International Historical Park was formally established by the United States and Canada in 1998, the year of the centennial of the [gold rush](/wiki/Klondike_Gold_Rush "Klondike Gold Rush") the park commemorates. The park comprises [Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park](/wiki/Klondike_Gold_Rush_National_Historical_Park "Klondike Gold Rush National Historical Park") in [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") (above) and [Chilkoot Trail National Historic Site](/wiki/Chilkoot_Trail "Chilkoot Trail") in British Columbia. Tens of thousands of prospectors took this trail in hopes of making their fortunes in the [Klondike River](/wiki/Klondike_River "Klondike River") district of [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon").
